nativeRange.habitat: Found on coral and rocky reefs, typically at depths of 5 to 20 m, though the species has been recorded as shallow as 3 m and as deep as 70 m. It is a reef-associated species that does not undertake migrations.

diet.notes: In the wild, the Banded Butterflyfish feeds on polychaete worms, coral polyps, crustaceans, and mollusc eggs. In captivity it generally requires a varied diet of meaty foods: frozen mysis shrimp, finely chopped shrimp, clam or mussel offered on the half shell, brine shrimp, and small crustaceans. Newly imported specimens often refuse prepared foods at first and may need live mysis or fresh seafood to be coaxed into eating. Multiple feedings per day are generally advised.

avoidWith: ornamental shrimp and crabs (will be eaten)
stony and soft corals (will nip polyps)
very aggressive triggers or large damsels (will stress the butterflyfish)
conspecifics outside of an established mated pair

summary: A peaceful Western Atlantic butterflyfish marked with two broad black body bars and a third bar through the dorsal fin. Generally not considered reef-safe and tends to be a finicky feeder on arrival, making it best suited to a mature fish-only or FOWLR aquarium.
careNotes:
The Banded Butterflyfish is a Western Atlantic and Caribbean species that has been reported from Massachusetts south to Santa Catarina, Brazil, including the Gulf of Mexico and Saint Paul's Rocks. It is generally considered moderately demanding to keep: a mature display aquarium with stable water chemistry, plenty of live rock offering crevices and caves for shelter, and ample open swimming space tend to give the best results.

Adults reach around 16 cm, are active swimmers across all levels of the water column, and are often kept as a bonded pair, so a tank of at least 450 litres with a footprint of roughly 150 cm in length is generally recommended to give the fish adequate room.

The Banded Butterflyfish is generally not considered reef-safe. It has been reported to feed on tubeworms, feather dusters, clams (including Tridacna giant clams), and to nip at the polyps of stony and soft corals. It is best housed in a fish-only or FOWLR (fish-only with live rock) system. Multiple specimens can sometimes be kept together if introduced simultaneously, but established pairs should not be separated.

Juveniles display a distinct color phase that includes a large, ringed black ocellus — an eyespot — near the rear of the body in addition to the characteristic body bars; this marking fades as the fish matures. Like many butterflyfishes, the species is described as forming monogamous pairs in the wild.

Feeding can be challenging in the first weeks after arrival. Newly imported specimens often refuse prepared foods and may need live mysis shrimp, fresh clam or mussel offered on the half shell, or other meaty seafood to be coaxed into eating. Multiple feedings per day are generally advised, particularly during the acclimation period.
breedingNotes: The Banded Butterflyfish is an egg-scattering (oviparous) species that releases its eggs into open water (pelagic spawning) as a monogamous pair. It has not been reliably bred in home aquaria, and almost all specimens in the trade are wild-collected.

additional.name: reefapp.net
additional.notes: Additional cross-check on adult size (16 cm), temperament (peaceful), reef compatibility (not reef-safe; nips Tridacna), and the difficulty of getting newly added specimens to start feeding.

fish.breedingNotes: Spawns as monogamous pairs, releasing eggs into open water. Not known to have been bred in home aquaria.
